name: Lares
description: The Lares were Roman household deities, protectors of the home and family. Unlike major Olympian gods, Lares were intimately connected to the daily lives of individuals and families. Their worship was a deeply personal and localized practice, centered around the lararium, a shrine typically located in the atrium of a Roman house. These deities were not viewed as distant figures but as benevolent guardians, ensuring the well-being and prosperity of the household. Offerings of food, drink, and incense were made regularly to appease and honor them. The Lares were often depicted as young men dressed in short tunics, holding rhyta (libation vessels) and patera (offering dishes). Their presence symbolized the continuity of the family lineage and the sacredness of the domestic space. While each household had its own Lares Familiares, there were also Lares Publici, deities who protected the entire community and the state, highlighting the interconnectedness of the private and public spheres in Roman religion.
subtitle: Guardians of the Home

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Lares
keyMyths.description: While no single, canonical myth explains the origin of the Lares, various traditions suggest connections to ancestral spirits or deified heroes. Some accounts link them to the souls of deceased family members who continue to watch over their descendants. Others propose that they were originally agricultural deities, protecting fields and harvests before becoming associated with the home. Regardless of their exact origin, the Lares represent the enduring power of family and the importance of honoring one's ancestors.

keyMyths.title: The Founding of Rome and the Lares
keyMyths.description: According to legend, Romulus and Remus, the founders of Rome, were raised by a she-wolf and then adopted by a shepherd family. This humble upbringing highlights the importance of the family and the home, values deeply associated with the Lares. As protectors of the home, the Lares can be seen as embodying the spirit of Rome's origins, representing the simple virtues and close-knit community that formed the foundation of the city's greatness. Their worship reinforced the ideals of domestic harmony and familial loyalty, crucial for the stability of the Roman state.

keyMyths.title: The Compitalia Festival
keyMyths.description: The Compitalia was an annual festival celebrated in ancient Rome in honor of the Lares Compitales, the Lares of the crossroads. During this festival, offerings were made at shrines located at intersections throughout the city, symbolizing the protection of the community as a whole. The Compitalia involved processions, games, and feasts, bringing people together in a spirit of communal celebration. The festival reinforced the idea that the well-being of individual households was interconnected with the prosperity of the entire community, highlighting the importance of social cohesion and mutual support.
